quote: I'm not gonna put it on my roof.
stewart_read: Ethan spent 15 seconds narrating a Google Maps roof assessment before asking a single question about installation preferences. Carlin's correction is the direct consequence. Strategic read: Ethan was buying time while pulling up the address. Reactive read: the narration was filler that telegraphed a script assumption the customer hadn't confirmed. The reactive read holds — the narration created a false premise Carlin had to correct, costing rapport and redirecting the call.
alternative_hypothesis: Ethan was using the Google Maps pull as a natural transition to the interest question — the narration was intentional rapport-building, not script-running.
coaching_implication: Ask Ethan: 'Before you pulled up the roof, what did you know about where Carlin wanted to put the panels?' The lesson is to ask installation preference before assessing the roof, not after. A single question at 01:19 — 'Do you have a spot in mind for the panels?' — changes the entire trajectory of this call.

quote: I need to know how much it's gonna cost, then I'll know what direction to go.
stewart_read: This is the codex's price_estimate_before_design trigger verbatim. The canonical rebuttal exists: redirect to 'let me get you an actual design with real numbers — not just a random ballpark.' Ethan instead deferred to a future callback ('we'd have to wait until that's completed'), which is procedurally accurate but misses the curiosity-creation opportunity entirely. The rebuttal's job here was to keep Carlin engaged long enough to surface the bill and the electrical loads — which Carlin then volunteered anyway at 05:15 and 05:37.
alternative_hypothesis: Ethan's deferral was strategically correct because the structure genuinely isn't built yet — pricing without a mount location is impossible, so the callback is the honest answer.
coaching_implication: The deferral is honest but passive. Walk Ethan through the price_estimate_before_design rebuttal and ask: 'What would it have looked like to redirect Carlin toward curiosity about his own numbers rather than a future callback?' The goal isn't to fabricate a price — it's to make Carlin want to see his specific design.

quote: I've got heaters on them right now, it takes me to get down right about 10 degrees before the things they all shivering all over.
stewart_read: Carlin is describing active, ongoing electrical loads — chicken-coop heaters running through a Virginia winter. This is the most concrete bill-anchor opportunity in the call. The customer is not talking abstractly about solar; he is describing real current consumption that solar could offset. Ethan's response ('That's good.') is a filler acknowledgment that drops the anchor entirely. The codex's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip' — Ethan did none of the three.
coaching_implication: Show Ethan this moment in the recording. Ask: 'What question could you have asked here?' A suggested direction (not a fabricated line): something that connects the heater cost to what Carlin is already paying and what solar could do about it. The bill-anchor opportunity was handed to Ethan; he set it down.

quote: I also got, two twenty in my greenhouse running that so that my plants won't freeze.
stewart_read: A 220V greenhouse is a discrete secondary structure with its own electrical load — a direct match for the codex's TBD item 'solar_for_secondary_structure.' This is separate from the chicken-run discussion: Carlin has now described two non-roof structures with active electrical loads. Ethan's response is silence followed by 'Okay. Well, yeah, and that's—' before Carlin cuts back in. No bridge to Ion's capabilities, no curiosity question about the greenhouse's energy cost.
coaching_implication: Flag for Kenny as a concrete example to populate cross_sell_signals with the secondary-structure trigger. For Ethan: the greenhouse 220V line was an opening to ask what that load costs and whether Carlin had thought about powering it independently. Even if the call was heading toward disqualification, this question keeps the customer talking and surfaces real data.

quote: I got all kinds of ideas. I just need some prices so I know which way to go.
stewart_read: The pricing request recurs. Ethan's adjustment from the first touch (03:19) moved in the wrong direction: he offered roof-mount as the only available pricing path ('the only way that we'd be able to really figure out a price is to look into a roof mounted installation'), which was a path Carlin had explicitly closed at 02:31. The adjustment addressed the pricing request by narrowing options rather than creating curiosity. This is the second touch of the multi-touch pricing pattern.
alternative_hypothesis: Ethan was being honest about Ion's actual constraints — without a confirmed mount location, a real design is impossible, and offering roof-mount was the only legitimate path available.
coaching_implication: The honesty is correct; the direction is wrong. Ethan's move re-opened a door Carlin had already closed, which accelerated the disqualification rather than extending the conversation. The coaching question: 'What would it have looked like to meet Carlin where he was — multiple structures, multiple ideas — and redirect toward what a design could show him, rather than narrowing to the one path he'd already rejected?'

quote: Yeah. I'll try calling some other time then.
stewart_read: No appointment, no locked callback time, no commitment anchor. 'I'll try calling some other time' is a soft exit — the codex's soft_outcome_distinction flags the major delta between callbacks with locked times versus 'we'll touch base.' Carlin's response ('give me a holler back... most time I don't know where I answer the phone') confirms the callback has near-zero show probability. Note: the audio degradation is a real confounding factor here — Ethan may have been unable to execute a proper close-out through the static. But the absence of any attempt to lock a time before the connection fully degraded is the gap.
alternative_hypothesis: The audio quality made any structured close-out impossible — Ethan was managing a failing connection, not choosing to skip the commitment anchor.
coaching_implication: Even with bad audio, the move is to attempt a time anchor before the line drops: 'Before we lose the connection — can I call you back [specific day/time]?' Ask Ethan what he would do differently if he had 10 seconds before the line went dead.

quote: I get tired of people keep on calling me, calling me with a junk call.
stewart_read: Carlin is not challenging Ethan's data sourcing — he is volunteering a trust signal. He distinguishes this call ('I'm interested in this call') from the spam calls he ignores. This is a trust gift: the customer is telling the rep why he answered and what makes this interaction different. Ethan's response is 'Mhmm' — a minimal acknowledgment that neither receives the gift nor builds on it. The codex classifies this as a trust gift requiring acknowledgment and a bridge to value.
coaching_implication: Show Ethan the contrast: Carlin says 'I'm interested in this call' and Ethan says 'Mhmm.' Ask: 'What could you have said here that would have honored what Carlin just told you?' A suggested direction: acknowledge the distinction Carlin drew, then use it to re-anchor the call's purpose — even if the commercial outcome is already closed, this moment is a relationship deposit that affects whether Carlin answers the next call.

quote: Okay. I'm I'm I'm fooling around with different, ideas.
stewart_read: Carlin is describing an active DIY energy project in progress — panels already installed ('I put a couple panels of tint on it already'), geothermal AC concepts, chicken-run solar, greenhouse power. This is a customer who is not casually curious about solar; he is already building toward it. The codex's interest_in_solar item calls for 60+ seconds of depth here. Ethan's response is 'Uh-huh. Okay. Yeah. That's cool.' — a filler that neither validates the enthusiasm nor extracts the bill-pain anchor that would make the project financially concrete.
coaching_implication: This is the verify.interest_in_solar depth gap in its clearest form. Carlin handed Ethan 90 seconds of unprompted enthusiasm about energy projects. The coaching question: 'What question could you have asked that would have connected Carlin's project energy to what he's currently paying?' The bill-anchor opportunity was embedded in this enthusiasm — Ethan needed one question to surface it.
